output:
Below is a comprehensive review and explanation of your situation. I’ll walk you through the federal and state rules that apply, point out the discrepancies we’ve found on your returns, and explain step‐by‐step how to correct the issues and prevent similar errors in the future.

──────────────────────────────
1. EXPLAINING THE APPLICABLE FEDERAL & STATE TAX LAWS

A. Reporting Self‐Employment Income  
• Federal law requires that all income from self‐employment (including freelance or contract work reported on a 1099‑NEC) be reported in full. You must report the entire amount you received—even if some of it isn’t subject to withholding—on Form 1040.  
• In your case, you actually earned $4,200 from freelance work, but your Form 1040 already shows only $2,400. This income shortfall must be corrected.  
• You generally use Schedule C (Profit or Loss from Business) to report this income and any allowable business expenses, and then calculate your net profit. Because you have net earnings from self‑employment, you must also file Schedule SE to compute your self‑employment tax.

B. Deductibility of Mortgage Interest  
• Mortgage interest is deductible as an itemized deduction on Schedule A if you are not claiming the standard deduction.  
• However, if your total itemized deductions (which might include state/local taxes, charitable contributions, etc.) do not exceed your standard deduction (which for a Single filer in the applicable tax year has a specific amount determined by the IRS), you may want to take the standard deduction.  
• In your file the standard deduction has been pre‑entered on Form 1040 (Line 12). That calculation may be more beneficial if your other itemized expenses (including the $9,800 in mortgage interest) don’t add up to exceed the standard deduction amount.

C. Dependent Qualification Rules  
• For federal purposes, a “qualifying child” must be related to you, be under age 19 (or under age 24 if a full‑time student), and generally not provide more than half of their own support during the year.  
• In your situation, you’re listing a 22‑year‑old full‑time college student as a dependent. While the age and student status can qualify, you also must meet the support test. If that individual earned $18,000 and provided over half of his or her own support, they would not be a qualifying child.  
• Alternatively, a “qualifying relative” has different criteria (including income limits) and generally must have gross income below a threshold—but then you also must provide more than half of their total support.  
• It’s very important that you review the support details to ensure you’re legally allowed to claim this person. Given the $18,000 earned, you should verify who actually provided the majority of the support.

D. The Correct Standard Deduction for Your Filing Status  
• For a Single filer, the IRS sets a standard deduction that is updated annually. (For example, recent years have seen amounts approximately in the $12,950 to $13,850 range, but you should verify for the tax year in question.)  
• Since your return has a pre‑entered standard deduction amount on Line 12 of Form 1040, make sure that it corresponds to the correct figure for your filing year. If it is incorrect, you’ll need to adjust it.

──────────────────────────────
2. REVIEWING YOUR FORMS & CHECKING FOR ACCURACY

A. Income Reporting  
• W‑2 Income: You reported $68,500 which appears to be correct if it matches your W‑2.  
• Self‑Employment Income: The 1099‑NEC shows $4,200, but only $2,400 has been entered on Form 1040. That’s a clear misreporting. The entire $4,200 must be reported.  
• Recalculate your total income:  
  W‑2: $68,500  
  + Freelance: $4,200  
  = Total Income: $72,700  
This total then flows into your AGI (after any adjustments are made).

B. Deductions and Mortgage Interest  
• Mortgage Interest: You have $9,800 in mortgage interest payments. This figure would normally be reported on Form 1098 and then entered on Schedule A if you are itemizing.  
• However, because the standard deduction is already entered and may be higher than the total of your allowable itemized deductions, you must decide which method results in lower taxable income.  
• Verify that the standard deduction on your return is the correct current amount for a Single filer.

C. Calculations on Your State Return  
• You noted that your state tax liability does not match the AGI shown on the federal return. Many state tax formulas start with federal AGI and then require modifications.  
• The discrepancy may be due to the incorrect self‑employment income. Once that error is corrected (the AGI increases), the state return calculations will change.  
• Also, note that some states have their own rules regarding the treatment of deductions like mortgage interest. Make sure that your state's calculation conforms with state law once you fix the AGI.

──────────────────────────────
3. GUIDANCE FOR CORRECT FORM COMPLETION

A. Required Forms and Schedules  
• Form 1040: Correct your gross income to include the full $4,200 from freelance work.  
• Schedule C: Report all freelance/contract income ($4,200) along with any business expenses you may have incurred.  
• Schedule SE: Calculate your self‑employment tax based on the net profit from Schedule C.  
• Schedule A (if you decide to itemize): Report the $9,800 mortgage interest (plus any other itemized deductions you have). Compare the total with your standard deduction. In many cases, a taxpayer with no additional itemizable deductions would benefit more from the standard deduction.  
• State Return: Begin with the corrected federal AGI. Then make sure all state‐specific adjustments are applied correctly.

B. Data Entry Corrections  
• On Form 1040, change the freelance income entry from $2,400 to $4,200.  
• Review and update the AGI, taxable income, and tax calculations accordingly. Then cross‑check the computed value against your state return adjustments.  
• Double‑check that the correct standard deduction (for your filing year and single status) is used. If the pre‑filled amount is wrong, update it to the IRS mandated amount for that year.

C. Itemizing Versus Standard Deduction  
• You need to decide: Do your itemized deductions (which, based solely on $9,800 in mortgage interest, may be less than the standard deduction) outweigh the standard deduction?  
• For most Single filers with only mortgage interest and no additional large itemized deductions (e.g., property taxes, charitable contributions, etc.), the standard deduction is likely more beneficial.

──────────────────────────────
4. ANSWERING YOUR QUESTIONS & PREVENTATIVE ADVICE

A. Claiming the Dependent  
• Legally claiming the 22‑year‑old college student as a dependent depends on whether you provided more than half of their support. Although they are a full‑time student and under 24 (which is promising), the fact that they earned $18,000 means you need to carefully compare their personal contributions to support versus your financial contributions.  
• Review your records of expenses (tuition, room and board, etc.) to determine if you indeed covered over 50% of their support. If not, you cannot claim them. Documentation is crucial here.

B. Federal vs. State Figures  
• The differences you see between your federal AGI and the state’s computed tax liability likely stem from the error in self‑employment income and/or state modifications.  
• Once you correct the federal figures (especially the income from freelance work), recalculate the state tax liability. Keep in mind that states sometimes require add‑backs or subtractions that can cause differences from the federal AGI.

C. Avoiding Issues Next Year  
• Recordkeeping: Keep all 1099‑NEC and W‑2 forms, receipts for business expenses, mortgage interest statements, and documentation of any support you provide to dependents.  
• Estimated Tax Payments: Since self‑employment income is not subject to withholding, consider making quarterly estimated tax payments to avoid penalties and a large tax bill at year‑end.  
• Double‑Check Entries: Before finalizing your return, review that every income source is fully reported. A small error (like entering $2,400 instead of $4,200) can cascade into AGI, tax liability, and even state return errors.

D. Recommendations for Correct Completion  
• Amend the Form 1040: Increase your freelance income to $4,200.  
• Include Schedule C and Schedule SE for your self‑employment income.  
• Confirm whether to itemize by comparing your total itemized deductions (including the $9,800 mortgage interest) vs. the standard deduction. In your situation, the standard deduction likely provides a greater benefit.  
• Recalculate your AGI and taxable income on both federal and state returns once these corrections are made.

──────────────────────────────
SUMMARY

— You must correct the freelance income so that the full $4,200 is reported using Schedule C and then compute self‑employment tax with Schedule SE.  
— Mortgage interest is deductible if you itemize on Schedule A, but for most taxpayers the standard deduction (properly set for a Single filer) is more advantageous unless your itemized deductions are significantly higher.  
— Confirm that you can legally claim the 22‑year‑old college student by reviewing the support test; their $18,000 income may indicate independence if you did not cover more than half their support.  
— Update your state return once your federal figures are accurate; state tax calculations may need adjustments based on the corrected AGI.  
— For the future, maintain detailed records and consider estimated quarterly tax payments for non‑wage income.

By following this guidance, you’ll ensure that your federal and state returns are completed accurately and that you’re taking all allowable deductions and credits while avoiding errors that can lead to future headaches or penalties. Should you have any further questions or require additional clarification, please feel free to ask.
